author: @jadenephrite
text: Thank you for your video. For those who are curious, the small images and writing on coins are engraved with the aid of machines. For example, in order to aid a coin engraver, an apparatus known as the Janvier Reducing Machine was used. It was a pantograph which used a stylus to trace the contours from a pattern model. On the other end, it had an engraving router which carved out an image of the pattern onto a coin stamping die made of steel. In antiquity, coin stamping dies were engraved by hand. In modern times, Computer Numerical Control (CNC) machines have replaced the Janvier engraving pantograph.

author: @disklamer
text:
Lovely device, with so many uses. I really enjoy your revisiting of these age old crafting tools!
I've used what was sold as a toy pantograph to trace circuitboards to transparanet film in order to figure out the schematic, to copy ornaments from books to pieces of wood for carving, to copy lettering for posters and to make patterns for upholstery. 
The accuracy is limited but it's a fantastic guide to overall proportions and shapes.
I found that the angle at which you operate the device matters a lot towards how easy it wants to move along, and how precise it is. You kind of want to avoid the right angles, I ended up using 60° or so.
Interestingly the principle of the mechanism, moving in 3d, is also used to this day in the manufacture of wooden airplane propellers.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it is still used and nice advice there for using it mate - cheers
